title: Long-range interactions assisted shortcuts to adiabaticity and battery charging in open quantum critical systems
abstract: In this work we show that long-range interactions can be significantly beneficial for implementing shortcuts to adiabaticity (STA) in many-body open quantum critical systems driven out of equilibrium, as well as for charging quantum batteries in the presence of dissipation. In sharp contrast to short range interactions where passage through criticality may demand STA control with non-zero interactions between infinitely distant spins, using the example of a Kitaev chain with long-range couplings, we find that the corresponding control may involve involve interaction strength with decays algebraically with distance. In case of non-unitary control, the advantage of long-range interactions manifest through reduction in the cost of STA. We further propose a modified STA technique aimed at charging a quantum battery in the presence of dissipation, in which case long-range interactions may enhance the resultant ergotropy. Our results establish long-range interactions as a valuable resource for quantum control, with direct implications for quantum technologies.
summary: The paper demonstrates that long-range interactions are a valuable resource for controlling open quantum critical systems. By analyzing energy costs and ergotropy, the authors show that LRIs significantly enhance both Shortcuts to Adiabaticity implementation and the charging capability of quantum batteries compared to nearest-neighbor models.
main_problem: The study investigates how long-range interactions (LRIs) can enhance quantum control protocols, specifically Shortcuts to Adiabaticity (STA), in open quantum critical systems and improve the charging efficiency of quantum batteries under dissipation.
main_result: LRIs provide a significant advantage over short-range interactions by reducing the cost of STA control and enhancing the resultant ergotropy during quantum battery charging near certain critical points.
method: The analysis involves deriving counterdiabatic Hamiltonians in momentum space, calculating energy costs (heat/power), and modeling dissipation using Lindblad master equations for open systems.
model_or_system: The model is a Long-Range Kitaev (LRK) chain of spinless fermions coupled to a fermionic bath. The dynamics are governed by the Hamiltonian $H_0(t)$ which includes long-range coupling terms decaying as $|i-j|^{-\alpha}$.
key_observables: Heat current ($	ext{I}_{	ext{STA}}$), power ($P_{	ext{STA}}$), ergotropy ($W_{	ext{tot}}(t)$), and the counterdiabatic coupling strength ($h_m$).
important_parameters: Interaction decay exponent $\alpha$ (defining long-range vs. short-range regimes), critical points ($\mu = \pm 1$), and temperature/inverse temperature ($ eta$).
main_assumptions: The system is coupled to a fermionic bath, leading to an instantaneous thermal state; calculations often rely on low-momentum expansions near criticality.
figures_summary: Figures compare the analytical and numerical results for the CD coupling strength $h_m(\mu, \alpha)$ across different interaction regimes ($\alpha$) and critical points ($\mu=-1$ vs. $\mu=+1$).
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes STA protocols in open systems, first deriving the necessary counterdiabatic terms ($h_m$), then calculating energy costs (heat/power), and finally applying these concepts to optimize quantum battery charging via ergotropy enhancement.
why_interesting: This work bridges non-equilibrium thermodynamics, quantum control theory, and condensed matter physics by showing how interaction topology (long-range vs. short-range) dictates the feasibility and efficiency of advanced quantum protocols like STA.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Comparison between the numerically evaluated CD coupling hm(µ, α) and the analytical predictions in the long-range regime. The main panel shows the behavior close to criticality µ = −1 (ϵ−= 0.3, α = 1.4); the CD coupling exhibits a crossover from a detuned asymptotic decay described by Eq. (21) to a power-law behavior Eq. 22, controlled by the characteristic length scale mϵ = (Aα/|ϵ|)1/(α−1). The inset shows the critical scaling at ϵ−= 0 for α = 1.4 and α = 1.6 (see Eq. (23)). (b) Comparison between numerical and analytical results for the CD coupling in the short-range regime, showing exponentially localized behavior close criticality µ = −1 (see Eq. 24) (main panel) and the...
Figure 2. Dependence of hm(µ, α), obtained through numer- ical integration of Eq. (18), on the interaction exponent α near µ = ±1. Here ϵ± = 0.1.

Figure 3. (a)–(d) Main panels show the transition rates γ(k) mn(t) as a function of the time-dependent chemical potential µ(t) driven across the critical point µ = −1, for k = 0.1). The insets display γ(k) mn(t) with µ(t) driven across the critical point µ = +1, for k = 3.0. Results are presented for different values of the LRI exponent α in low temperature regime β = 10. (e),(f) Same as above, but in the high-temperature regime β = 0.01.

Figure 4. (a) Heat current ISTA in the low-temperature regime β = 10. (b) Heat current ISTA (main) and power PSTA (inset) in the high-temperature regime β = 0.1. (c) Power PSTA in the low-temperature regime β = 10.

Figure 5. (a) Ergotropy W(t) with µ for different values of α at inverse temperature β = 1. The ergotropy increases with increasing range of interactions. (b) Variation of the ergotropy W(t) with µ for different values of β, for α = 1.2. Lower temperatures result in higher values of ergotropy.
